nativeRange.habitat: Mid-water to lower zones of larger rivers and their tributaries; favours areas with moderate current, submerged roots, driftwood, and rocky substrate

decorPreferences: driftwood and twisted roots for shelter and territory breaks
hardy epiphyte plants only (Anubias, Java fern, Microsorium tied to hardscape — soft-leaved plants will be eaten)
moderate current via powerhead or spray bar

diet.notes: Strongly plant-biased; soft-leaved aquatic plants are consumed voraciously and should be considered unavailable in any tank housing this species. Base diet of high-quality vegetable-based flake, spirulina pellets, algae wafers, and blanched greens (zucchini, cucumber, spinach, lettuce). Supplement with frozen or live bloodworm, Daphnia, and brine shrimp two to three times per week. Fruit pieces (melon, mango) are accepted and appreciated. Varied diet supports condition and reduces plant-nibbling pressure on hardscape vegetation.

goodWith: medium-to-large South American cichlids (severums, festivums, eartheaters)
other Anostomidae of similar size (with caution and ample space)
avoidWith: small or delicate fish (tetras, rasboras, dwarf corydoras) — harassment risk
long-finned varieties of any species — fin-nipping likely
live plants (soft-leaved plants destroyed; species-only or Anubias/Java fern tanks recommended)
conspecifics in groups of 2–7 — intense hierarchical fighting; keep singly or in groups of 8+
Spotted Headstander (Chilodus punctatus, fw-fish-229) — confused at point of sale but that species is peaceful and plant-safe; incompatible temperaments
Striped Headstander (Anostomus anostomus, fw-fish-231) — both are plant-eaters and semi-aggressive; territory conflicts likely without very large footprint

summary: The Marbled Headstander is a striking mid-sized Amazonian characin that adopts a characteristic 45° head-down posture at rest and while foraging. An aggressive plant-eater, it destroys soft-leaved vegetation; only hard epiphytes survive. Conspecific aggression is high in small groups — keep singly or in groups of eight or more in a large, well-decorated tank.
careNotes:
Abramites hypselonotus is widely distributed across the Amazon, Orinoco, and Paraguay–Paraná river systems of South America, and is one of the more commonly encountered headstanders in the hobby, though it is frequently misidentified at point of sale. It is distinctly different from the peaceful, plant-safe Spotted Headstander (Chilodus punctatus) and should not be confused with it. The characteristic head-down, 45° body posture is a natural resting and foraging position shared with other Anostomidae, not a sign of stress.

Plant destruction is the most important husbandry consideration: essentially all soft-leaved aquatic plants will be eaten, often overnight. Only hard epiphytes — Anubias, Java fern, Bolbitis, or Microsorum — attached to driftwood or rocks will survive long-term. Any planted aquarium concept should be abandoned for this species; biotope décor of driftwood, smooth rocks, and sandy substrate is more appropriate and more representative of its natural habitat.

Conspecific aggression is the second major consideration. In groups of two to seven individuals, a dominant fish will relentlessly pursue and injure subordinates. The two safe configurations are: a single specimen, or a group of eight or more where aggression is diffused across multiple individuals and no single fish is constantly targeted. A minimum tank footprint of 120 × 45 cm (200 L) is appropriate for a single fish; groups of eight or more require at least 300–400 L with substantial broken sight lines via driftwood.

Towards heterospecifics the species is semi-aggressive: robust, similarly sized South American cichlids, silver dollars, and large catfish make acceptable tankmates. Small or delicate species and any long-finned fish should be excluded. Water quality should be maintained carefully — soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 5.5–7.5, gH 1–10) mirrors Amazonian blackwater and clearwater conditions; temperatures of 24–26 °C are optimal. Dissolved oxygen should be kept high via surface agitation. This species has not been bred successfully in captivity.
breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding records exist. In the wild, pairs are reported to scatter adhesive eggs over dense vegetation. The lack of reliable captive breeding data means sexing criteria, triggering conditions, and fry care are essentially unknown in the hobby context. Not considered a viable breeding project for home aquarists.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Abramites hypselonotus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (20–30°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (18–268 ppm ≈ gH 1–15), minimum tank base 120 × 30 cm, native range (Amazon, Orinoco, Paraguay-Paraná across Bolivia, Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Guyana, Peru, Venezuela, Paraguay, Argentina), diet (opportunistic omnivore with strong plant-eating tendency), grouping (large group in large tank), breeding (no captive records), and fin-nipping / plant-destruction warnings.

additional.name: FishBase — Abramites hypselonotus
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (14.0 cm TL), temperature (23–27°C), pH (6.0–7.5), taxonomy (Anostomidae, Characiformes), distribution (Orinoco, Amazon, Paraguay, lower Paraná), IUCN Least Concern status, trophic level 2.9.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Marbled Headstander
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature 23–27°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness up to 18 dH, swim zone mid-water (oblique/head-down angle), group size 8+ to distribute aggression, diet (flake, pellets, frozen foods plus vegetable matter), plant-eating advisory, no captive breeding records, native range including Uruguay.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Abramites hypselonotus
additional.notes: Used for cross-reference of taxonomic synonyms (Abramites ternetzi, Abramites microcephalus) and general distribution data.

fish.breedingNotes: No confirmed captive breeding records. Wild pairs scatter eggs over dense vegetation. Triggering conditions and fry care unknown.
